abstract: Stimuli-responsive hydrogels capable of programmable shape transformations are vital components for the advancement of soft robotics, 4D printing, and intelligent biomedical systems. Despite their potential, these technologies increasingly demand hydrogel architectures that are not only structurally complex and heterogeneous, but also modular and reconfigurable. In this work, we demonstrate a method for constructing such advanced hydrogel assemblies by reversibly bonding discrete units using a light-controlled metallopolymer adhesive. This adhesive facilitates robust interfacial attachment through a combination of metal–ligand coordination and polymer chain entanglement. By integrating units that respond to temperature and pH, we developed assemblies capable of sophisticated, multi-stimuli-responsive motions and shape-shifting behaviors. Uniquely, these structures can be disassembled under light irradiation and subsequently reassembled or reconfigured via thermal treatment, allowing for the flexible reprogramming of their functional properties. The use of reversible adhesives to build and remodel hydrogel systems offers a versatile framework for designing next-generation intelligent materials and soft robots with customizable, user-defined capabilities.
